questions.answer: A. The number of leaf nodes in the tree
questions.to_find: What does the function DoSomething() return when called with the root of a non-empty binary tree?

understand.plain: The function DoSomething() is a recursive function that traverses a binary tree. For each node, if it is a leaf (both children are NULL), it returns 1. Otherwise it recursively adds up the counts from left and right subtrees. This accumulates the count of leaf nodes.

keywords.explain: A node in a binary tree that has no children (both leftChild and rightChild are NULL).
keywords.example: In a tree with root A having children B and C where B and C have no children, B and C are leaf nodes.

keywords.explain: A function that calls itself on smaller sub-problems to compute a result for the whole problem.
keywords.example: DoSomething(root) = DoSomething(left) + DoSomething(right) for internal nodes.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" width="320" height="200" viewBox="0 0 320 200"><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><circle cx="160" cy="30" r="20" class="bg"/><text x="160" y="36" text-anchor="middle" class="lbl ink">A</text><circle cx="80" cy="90" r="20" class="bg"/><text x="80" y="96" text-anchor="middle" class="lbl ink">B</text><circle cx="240" cy="90" r="20" class="bg"/><text x="240" y="96" text-anchor="middle" class="lbl ink">C</text><circle cx="40" cy="155" r="20" class="hi"/><text x="40" y="161" text-anchor="middle" class="lbl ink">D</text><circle cx="120" cy="155" r="20" class="hi"/><text x="120" y="161" text-anchor="middle" class="lbl ink">E</text><circle cx="240" cy="155" r="20" class="hi"/><text x="240" y="161" text-anchor="middle" class="lbl ink">F</text><line x1="145" y1="46" x2="95" y2="74" stroke="#B4B2A9" stroke-width="2"/><line x1="175" y1="46" x2="225" y2="74" stroke="#B4B2A9" stroke-width="2"/><line x1="65" y1="106" x2="50" y2="136" stroke="#B4B2A9" stroke-width="2"/><line x1="95" y1="106" x2="110" y2="136" stroke="#B4B2A9" stroke-width="2"/><line x1="240" y1="110" x2="240" y2="136" stroke="#B4B2A9" stroke-width="2"/><text x="40" y="192" text-anchor="middle" class="sub mut">leaf=1</text><text x="120" y="192" text-anchor="middle" class="sub mut">leaf=1</text><text x="240" y="192" text-anchor="middle" class="sub mut">leaf=1</text><text x="160" y="185" text-anchor="middle" class="tag ink">Total = 3 leaf nodes</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: A binary tree with root A, internal nodes B and C, and leaf nodes D, E, F. Leaf nodes are highlighted. The function returns 3, counting the 3 leaf nodes.

given.aim: Determine what value DoSomething() computes for a binary tree.

terms.meaning: Returns 0; an empty subtree contributes 0 to the count.
terms.example: If a node has no left child, DoSomething(NULL) = 0.
terms.connects: Ensures recursion terminates at empty subtrees.

terms.meaning: Returns 1; this node is a leaf.
terms.example: A node with no children contributes exactly 1 to the count.
terms.connects: Each leaf adds 1 to the total.

terms.meaning: Returns sum of DoSomething on left and right subtrees.
terms.example: If left subtree has 2 leaves and right has 3, internal node returns 5.
terms.connects: Aggregates leaf counts from both sides.

given.plan: Trace the recursion: at each leaf, return 1; at each internal node, sum results from children. The total is the number of leaves.

steps.title: Analyze base and leaf cases
steps.formula_raw: if ptr == NULL: return 0; if leaf: return 1
steps.apply: When ptr is NULL (empty tree/subtree), value = 0 is returned. When both children are NULL, the node is a leaf and value = 1 is returned. So each leaf contributes exactly 1.
steps.note: This is the counting mechanism for leaves.

steps.title: Analyze recursive case for internal nodes
steps.apply: For an internal node (at least one non-NULL child), the function adds results from both subtrees. Since every path ultimately hits leaves returning 1 or NULLs returning 0, the sum accumulates exactly the number of leaf nodes in the entire tree.
steps.note: Internal nodes themselves do not contribute 1 directly; only leaves do.

steps.title: Conclude what is counted
steps.apply: The value returned by DoSomething(root) = total number of leaf nodes in the tree. It does NOT count internal nodes or the height.
steps.note: A common confusion is thinking it counts all nodes, but internal nodes only pass through sums without adding 1 for themselves.

solution.result: A. The number of leaf nodes in the tree

questions.formula_note: Recursive post-order traversal counting leaf nodes: each leaf contributes 1, internal nodes contribute the sum of their children's counts.

questions.question:
A scheme for storing binary trees in an array X is as follows. Indexing of X starts at 1 instead of 0. The root is stored at X[1]. For a node stored at X[i], the left child, if any, is stored in X[2i] and the right child, if any, is stored in X[2i+1]. To be able to store any binary tree on n vertices, the minimum size of X should be:

A. log_2(n)
B. n
C. 2n + 1
D. 2^n - 1
questions.to_find: The minimum array size needed to store any binary tree on n vertices using the given indexing scheme.

understand.plain: When a binary tree is stored in an array using the heap-like indexing (root at index 1, children of node at i stored at 2i and 2i+1), a skewed binary tree can cause very high indices. A right-skewed tree of n nodes will use indices 1, 3, 7, 15, ... so the last node sits at index 2^(n-1), meaning the array must have at least 2^n - 1 slots.

keywords.explain: Storing a binary tree in an array where node at index i has left child at 2i and right child at 2i+1.
keywords.example: Root at index 1, its left child at 2, right child at 3, left child of node 2 at index 4, etc.

keywords.explain: A binary tree where every node has only one child (all left or all right), forming a chain.
keywords.example: A right-skewed tree of 3 nodes: root (index 1) -> right child (index 3) -> right child (index 7). Even though there are only 3 nodes, index 7 is needed.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" width="340" height="220" viewBox="0 0 340 220"><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><text x="10" y="20" class="lbl ink">Right-skewed tree (n=4 nodes):</text><circle cx="60" cy="55" r="18" class="bg"/><text x="60" y="61" text-anchor="middle" class="sub ink">i=1</text><line x1="70" y1="70" x2="110" y2="95" stroke="#B4B2A9" stroke-width="2"/><circle cx="120" cy="110" r="18" class="bg"/><text x="120" y="116" text-anchor="middle" class="sub ink">i=3</text><line x1="130" y1="125" x2="175" y2="150" stroke="#B4B2A9" stroke-width="2"/><circle cx="185" cy="160" r="18" class="bg"/><text x="185" y="166" text-anchor="middle" class="sub ink">i=7</text><line x1="195" y1="175" x2="240" y2="195" stroke="#B4B2A9" stroke-width="2"/><circle cx="250" cy="200" r="18" class="hi"/><text x="250" y="206" text-anchor="middle" class="sub ink">i=15</text><text x="10" y="210" class="sub mut">Max index = 2^n - 1 = 15 for n=4</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: A right-skewed binary tree with 4 nodes using indices 1, 3, 7, 15. The last node uses index 15 = 2^4 - 1, so the array must have at least 2^n - 1 = 15 slots.

given.aim: Find the minimum array size to store any binary tree on n nodes using heap-style indexing.

terms.connects: All other indices are derived from this starting point.

terms.meaning: Left child of X[i] is at X[2i]; right child at X[2i+1].
terms.example: Node at index 4 has children at indices 8 and 9.
terms.connects: For a right-skewed tree, each step doubles the index plus 1.

terms.meaning: Worst case for array size: n nodes form a right-chain, using indices 1, 3, 7, 15, ..., 2^n - 1.
terms.example: 4-node right-skewed tree uses indices 1, 3, 7, 15. Max index = 15 = 2^4 - 1.
terms.connects: Determines the minimum required array size.

given.plan: Determine the maximum possible index for the n-th node across all possible binary tree shapes. A right-skewed tree always gives the largest index: the k-th node (all right children) sits at index 2^k - 1, so n nodes need array size at least 2^n - 1.

steps.title: Identify the worst-case tree shape
steps.apply: For a balanced complete binary tree, the maximum index for n nodes is roughly n. But for a right-skewed tree, each node takes only the right child slot: node 1 at index 1, node 2 at index 3 (= 2*1+1), node 3 at index 7 (= 2*3+1), node k at index 2^k - 1.
steps.note: The right-skewed tree maximizes array indices because we always go to the right child (2i+1) at each step.

steps.title: Compute the maximum index for n nodes
steps.formula_raw: Index of k-th node in right-skewed tree = 2^k - 1
steps.apply: For the n-th node in a right-skewed tree: index = 2^n - 1. So the array must be at least of size 2^n - 1 to accommodate this case.

steps.title: Verify the answer matches the options
steps.apply: Among the options: log_2(n) is too small; n is insufficient for skewed trees; 2n+1 is also insufficient (for n=4, 2n+1=9 but we need 15); 2^n - 1 is correct.

questions.formula_note: Heap-style array indexing: node at index i has children at 2i (left) and 2i+1 (right). Worst case is a right-skewed tree where the n-th node has index 2^n - 1.

questions.question:
The height of a binary tree is the maximum number of edges in any root to leaf path. The maximum number of nodes in a binary tree of height h is:

A. 2^h - 1
B. 2^(h-1) - 1
C. 2^(h+1) - 1
D. 2^(h+1)
questions.to_find: The maximum number of nodes in a binary tree of height h (where height = max edges in root-to-leaf path).

understand.plain: A binary tree's height is defined as the number of edges on the longest root-to-leaf path. A tree of height h has at most h+1 levels (levels 0 through h). To maximize nodes, every level should be completely filled. A complete binary tree with h+1 levels has 2^(h+1) - 1 nodes.

keywords.explain: Height = maximum number of edges from root to any leaf. A single-node tree has height 0.
keywords.example: A tree with root and two children has height 1 (one edge from root to leaf).

keywords.explain: A binary tree where every level is fully filled (each node has exactly 2 children except leaves).
keywords.example: A complete binary tree with 3 levels (height 2) has 1+2+4 = 7 nodes = 2^3 - 1.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" width="320" height="180" viewBox="0 0 320 180"><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><text x="5" y="18" class="sub mut">Level 0 (1 node)</text><circle cx="160" cy="35" r="16" class="bg"/><text x="160" y="41" text-anchor="middle" class="sub ink">1</text><text x="5" y="75" class="sub mut">Level 1 (2 nodes)</text><circle cx="100" cy="85" r="16" class="bg"/><text x="100" y="91" text-anchor="middle" class="sub ink">2</text><circle cx="220" cy="85" r="16" class="bg"/><text x="220" y="91" text-anchor="middle" class="sub ink">4</text><text x="5" y="130" class="sub mut">Level h (2^h nodes)</text><circle cx="60" cy="140" r="16" class="hi"/><circle cx="140" cy="140" r="16" class="hi"/><circle cx="200" cy="140" r="16" class="hi"/><circle cx="270" cy="140" r="16" class="hi"/><line x1="152" y1="50" x2="112" y2="70" stroke="#B4B2A9" stroke-width="2"/><line x1="168" y1="50" x2="208" y2="70" stroke="#B4B2A9" stroke-width="2"/><line x1="88" y1="100" x2="72" y2="125" stroke="#B4B2A9" stroke-width="2"/><line x1="112" y1="100" x2="128" y2="125" stroke="#B4B2A9" stroke-width="2"/><line x1="208" y1="100" x2="192" y2="125" stroke="#B4B2A9" stroke-width="2"/><line x1="232" y1="100" x2="258" y2="125" stroke="#B4B2A9" stroke-width="2"/><text x="160" y="170" text-anchor="middle" class="tag ink">Total = 2^(h+1) - 1</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: A complete binary tree with h+1 levels (0 to h). Level 0 has 1 node, level 1 has 2, ..., level h has 2^h nodes. Total = 1+2+4+...+2^h = 2^(h+1) - 1.

given.aim: Find the maximum number of nodes in a binary tree of height h (height = number of edges in longest root-to-leaf path).

terms.meaning: The longest root-to-leaf path has h edges, meaning h+1 nodes on that path.
terms.example: Height 0 means just a root node (1 node). Height 1 means root plus one level of children.
terms.connects: Determines the number of levels: there are h+1 levels (level 0 to level h).

terms.meaning: A tree of height h has h+1 levels (from level 0 at root to level h at deepest leaves).
terms.connects: Maximum nodes = sum of nodes at all levels when each level is fully filled.

terms.meaning: Level k of a full binary tree has 2^k nodes.
terms.example: Level 0: 1 node; level 1: 2 nodes; level 2: 4 nodes.
terms.connects: Summing from level 0 to h gives 2^(h+1) - 1 total nodes.

given.plan: A complete binary tree maximizes nodes. With h+1 levels (0 through h), the total is the geometric series: sum from k=0 to h of 2^k = 2^(h+1) - 1.

steps.title: Determine number of levels
steps.apply: Height h means there are edges from root to the deepest leaf, so there are h+1 levels: level 0 (root), level 1, ..., level h (deepest leaves).
steps.note: Note: if height were defined as number of nodes (not edges), the formula would differ. Here height = edges.

steps.title: Sum nodes in a complete binary tree
steps.apply: Level 0: 1 = 2^0 nodes; level 1: 2 = 2^1 nodes; ...; level h: 2^h nodes. Sum = 1 + 2 + 4 + ... + 2^h = 2^(h+1) - 1 (geometric series with ratio 2).
steps.note: This is the standard formula for the sum of a geometric series: sum_{k=0}^{h} 2^k = 2^(h+1) - 1.

questions.formula_note: Geometric series sum: sum_{k=0}^{h} 2^k = 2^(h+1) - 1. A complete binary tree of height h (edge-based) has 2^(h+1) - 1 nodes.

questions.question: The maximum number of binary trees that can be formed with three unlabeled nodes is:
questions.to_find: The maximum number of structurally distinct binary trees with 3 unlabeled nodes.

understand.plain: We need to count the number of distinct structural shapes a binary tree can take when it has exactly 3 nodes and the nodes are unlabeled (so only the shape/structure matters, not the content). There are exactly 5 such distinct binary tree shapes.

keywords.explain: A binary tree where only the structure (shape) matters, not the identity/label of nodes.
keywords.example: Two trees with the same shape but different node values count as one unlabeled tree.

keywords.explain: The number of distinct binary trees with n unlabeled nodes is the n-th Catalan number C_n = C(2n,n)/(n+1).
keywords.example: C_3 = C(6,3)/4 = 20/4 = 5. So there are 5 distinct binary trees with 3 nodes.

understand.visual_alt: Five distinct structural shapes of binary trees with 3 nodes: T1=full tree (root with two children); T2=root with left child which has left child (left-left skewed); T3=root with right child which has right child (right-right skewed); T4=root with left child which has right child; T5=root with right child which has left child.

given.aim: Count the number of distinct structural binary trees with 3 unlabeled nodes.

terms.meaning: C_n = C(2n,n)/(n+1); counts distinct binary trees with n unlabeled nodes.
terms.connects: For n=3 nodes, C_3 = 5 is the answer.

terms.meaning: Two binary trees are structurally distinct if one cannot be made identical to the other by relabeling nodes.
terms.example: A root with only a left child vs. a root with only a right child are two different shapes.
terms.connects: We enumerate all possible arrangements of left/right children for 3 nodes.

terms.meaning: A binary tree with n nodes can be split as: root + left subtree (k nodes) + right subtree (n-1-k nodes), for k = 0,1,...,n-1.
terms.example: For n=3: k=0: 1 tree (all right); k=1: 1 tree; k=2: 1 tree (all left); this gives 1+1+1=3 for skewed, but we must also count k=1 split both ways.
terms.connects: This recursion generates all 5 distinct shapes.

given.plan: Use the Catalan number formula C_n = C(2n,n)/(n+1) for n=3, OR enumerate all 5 distinct shapes directly by considering all ways to distribute 3 nodes in a binary tree structure.

steps.title: Apply Catalan number formula
steps.note: The n-th Catalan number gives the count of distinct binary tree shapes with n nodes.

steps.title: Enumerate the 5 distinct shapes
steps.apply: Shape 1: Root with left child and right child (balanced). Shape 2: Root -> left -> left (left-skewed). Shape 3: Root -> right -> right (right-skewed). Shape 4: Root -> left -> (left's right child). Shape 5: Root -> right -> (right's left child). These are all 5 structurally distinct binary trees with 3 nodes.
steps.note: Alternatively, by the recursive formula: T(3) = T(0)*T(2) + T(1)*T(1) + T(2)*T(0) = 1*2 + 1*1 + 2*1 = 5.

questions.formula_note: The number of distinct binary trees (unlabeled) with n nodes equals the n-th Catalan number: C_n = C(2n,n)/(n+1). For n=3, C_3 = 5.
